Cookbook | Recipes | Ingredients | Equipment | Techniques | Cookbook Disambiguation Pages | Ingredients  | Sweeteners

**Jaggery** or **gur** is a variety of unrefined sugar.[[1]](#cite_note-:0-1)[[2]](#cite_note-:1-2)[[3]](#cite_note-:2-3)[[4]](#cite_note-:3-4)

## Characteristics

[edit | edit source]

The sugar is made by boiling sap—either from sugarcane or various palm trees[[1]](#cite_note-:0-1)[[2]](#cite_note-:1-2)[[5]](#cite_note-:5-5)[[6]](#cite_note-:6-6)—in open kettles until concentrated and crystallizing.[[7]](#cite_note-:7-7)[[8]](#cite_note-:42-8)[[4]](#cite_note-:3-4)[[9]](#cite_note-:8-9) It is then transferred to molds and left to harden before unmolding.[[4]](#cite_note-:3-4) No molasses is washed from the sugar.[[3]](#cite_note-:2-3)

The color of jaggery can be anywhere from a light tan to a dark brown,[[1]](#cite_note-:0-1)[[5]](#cite_note-:5-5)[[9]](#cite_note-:8-9) with palm jaggery tending towards the dark end of the spectrum.[[5]](#cite_note-:5-5) The crystals tend to be coarse and the product crumbly.[[2]](#cite_note-:1-2)[[10]](#cite_note-:4-10) Because it is unrefined, jaggery contains concentrated flavor compounds and minerals, which gives it a rich flavor.[[5]](#cite_note-:5-5)[[6]](#cite_note-:6-6)[[8]](#cite_note-:42-8) The exact flavor depends on the source of the sap.[[5]](#cite_note-:5-5) When made from palm sap, the sugar is often called *gur* to differentiate it from the sugar cane product.[[6]](#cite_note-:6-6)[[10]](#cite_note-:4-10)

Source aside, jaggery can be found in a few different forms. At its simplest, it is found as a hard block or cone,[[3]](#cite_note-:2-3)[[7]](#cite_note-:7-7)[[5]](#cite_note-:5-5)[[9]](#cite_note-:8-9) but it may also come in a soft, almost spreadable consistency.[[2]](#cite_note-:1-2)[[7]](#cite_note-:7-7)[[6]](#cite_note-:6-6) Sometimes it is pre-crushed or grated into granules,[[5]](#cite_note-:5-5) which may be called *shakkar.[[4]](#cite_note-:3-4)* If washed and slightly refined, jaggery may also be called *khandsari.[[4]](#cite_note-:3-4)*

## Selection and storage

[edit | edit source]

If you have the time and equipment to break it down, purchasing jaggery in a block is perfectly acceptable. However, pre-ground jaggery is sometimes available and is more convenient in some applications.[[5]](#cite_note-:5-5)

Store jaggery in an airtight container away from light, heat, and moisture.[[5]](#cite_note-:5-5)

## Use

[edit | edit source]

Jaggery is most commonly produced and used in the cuisines of South and Southeast Asia,[[1]](#cite_note-:0-1)[[4]](#cite_note-:3-4)[[5]](#cite_note-:5-5)[[10]](#cite_note-:4-10) where it flavors both savory and sweet dishes.[[1]](#cite_note-:0-1)[[2]](#cite_note-:1-2)[[8]](#cite_note-:42-8)

## Substitution

[edit | edit source]

If you can't get jaggery specifically, other unrefined sugars may be used as a substitute. Be conscious, however, of the moisture level called for in your recipe, and make adjustments as necessary. Soft varieties of jaggery may be used instead of modern brown sugar.[[5]](#cite_note-:5-5)
